title: Joined as a frontend developer on their Project Delivery Model track meaning I had zero firm related obligations and was expected just to deliver client value via software development This is largely my experience after 3 years so the info was accurate Most of the projects I ve been on have been places to hone my development skills and do actual software development with competent teams and competent or hands off management All of my projects have been Federal or State level contracts My experience has been me being 100 focused on the projects themselves Deloitte itself generally stays in the background and I can focus on the webapp or whatever I m actually working on on the day to day which feels great The pay is good enough and I get 5 54hr PTO accrual every 2 weeks Holidays aren t generous imo We lose President s Day Veterans Day etc in exchange for having Dec 25 Jan 1st off every year Which is cool but really loads all of your holiday time off towards the Winter which is idk fine If you re on a good project and or are good at the technical work you do you won t be working high octane 8hr days with some exceptions It s chill and satisfying
pros: joined as a frontend developer on their project delivery model track meaning i had zero firm-related obligations and was expected just to deliver client value via software development this is largely my experience after 3 years so the info was accurate most of the projects i've been on have been places to hone my development skills and do actual software development with competent teams and competent or hands-off management all of my projects have been federal or state-level contracts my experience has been me being 100 focused on the projects themselves deloitte itself generally stays in the background and i can focus on the webapp or whatever i'm actually working on on the day-to-day which feels great the pay is good enough and i get 5 54hr pto accrual every 2-weeks holidays aren't generous imo we lose president's day veterans day etc in exchange for having dec 25-jan 1st off every year which is cool but really loads all of your holiday time off towards the winter which is idk fine if you're on a good project and or are good at the technical work you do you won't be working high-octane 8hr days with some exceptions it's chill and satisfying
cons: although technically a consultant i only do software dev work and am on an aggressive client-deliverable career track so my experience is from that perspective the experience here lives and dies by the project you're on and projects live and die by a combination of management quality and market conditions in 3 years i've been on 5 projects 2 ended due to mgmt incompetence and 2 ended due to market conditions unsurprisingly you can work with incredible people but as a software dev you have much less control over management conditions the company seems built around the non-technical consultants so folks like me get paid a steady salary and are best left alone to just build things but if you want to move up in the company get meaningful raises etc then you really can't be a software dev and have to pivot towards the kinds of roles that have people asking what does deloitte actually do what do you do all day meetings it's meetings by far the worst part of deloitte is more of a feature of software devs in consulting but nonetheless lack of job stability when you're hired you get put on one project for free if you choose to leave that project or more likely get cut then it is your responsibility to market yourself and find a new role within the company otherwise you're fired this is talked about as a positive or at least as something that fits in with consulting skills but if you're a software dev you probably don't have a wide network of folks to help you out because you're working on software not networking with non-technical folks so then you get to search through a constantly-changing but always bad internal marketplace app for a new role that hopefully was written edited by a technical person to find a new role befitting your technical skills you do an informal interview and transition to the new project and then hopefully do more software dev until the next suddenly risking unemployment crisis tl dr the software dev work itself is pretty good fulfilling interesting etc the stability and structure around getting to do the software dev you were hired for is very bad

title: An array of client companies in a variety of industries Will get experience in auditing as well as insight into how companies operate
pros: an array of client companies in a variety of industries will get experience in auditing as well as insight into how companies operate
cons: extreme working hours with no additional compensation i worked 3 straight months of 80 hour weeks one week i worked 90 hours saturdays and sundays included within my first 6 months with the firm in addition to the client work hours there are numerous hours of independent learning tasks that each professional must complete outside of their workday on their own time these are not so time-consuming when you are at the staff level but as you progress to senior level these become pretty significantly time-consuming you could be put on a client in a different state than where your home office is and have to travel via plane to the remote office for months on end this is not widely publicized so just be aware there is poor workforce deployment management and a dishonest representation of the seasonal nature of the workload they boast the idea that there is a 2-month busy season but that summers are extremely calm and you will be able to take advantage of their vacation policies almost whenever you like during the long calm period what they don't tell you is that busy seasons are based on client filing timelines which are happening at various times throughout the whole year based on the client there is always a busy season going on at any point during the year the reality is that they can and will move you from one busy season client to another to another so that you are constantly working well over the normal 40 hours and so that taking a vacation would be frowned upon and would not receive approval since your client is in its busy season please note that this happened to me when i knew of at least three other employees at my level who were on unassigned time and did not have a client there is a mindset with many of the higher employees at this location that harms their ability to train teach staff resulting in staff that are poorly developed for their roles when it comes to rising to higher levels within the company which occurs fairly quickly i and others of my teammates at my level even experienced a 3rd-year senior frequently giving blatantly incorrect instructions on tasks either because they didn't know what to do and wouldn't admit it or because they wanted to make the staff look bad and finally please don't let them convince you that this is a truly consistently apprenticeship style job for some it is but for others it is not
